So your question about how we would describe Fieldguide to someone outside of audit and advisory.
So Fieldguide, we are, I would say, to kind of paint the challenge.
Historically, the audit advisory industry has worked with sort of very outdated legacy tech.
So some of the competitors or existing solutions.
shot?

about,
And so as a result, there's a lot of siloed solutions that came up to solve.
So for instance, like ingesting data or exchanging documents and files with clients or data analytics type of solutions.
So as a result, these firms are using very like a fragmented and sort of tech stack that includes like both sort of the legacy tech and a bunch of these like new pointed solutions that came up.
And so that's sort of one of the core challenges that Fieldguide first wanted to solve for is to create.

This end-to-end platform that brings everything together in one place so that these firms don't have to use these different solutions for different parts of their workflows and they can just do everything within Fieldguide.
So from the start of their engagement to the end of their engagement and being able to work across the entire engagement team and also with their clients.
But on top of that, right, we are also, from the very beginning, we have built our platform with AI in mind.
And so we started with kind of incorporating gen AI capabilities within our platform.
And most recently, we have now embedded AI agents throughout our platform to help with different workflows throughout the engagement lifecycle.
That is kind of to help address some of the capacity challenges that our firms are facing, in addition to sort of that fragmented workflow, right?
These firms are getting...

You know, having to deal with more complex engagements, more complex clients, more data, and they're just not being able to sort of get the talent fast enough to help and address this demand for their services.
And so with AI, they could kind of offload some of the more manual and tedious tasks to automate some of that so that they could free up their time for more high-value services.
So in a nutshell, Fieldguide is essentially an end-to-end platform for audit and advisory firms with purpose-built agents embedded throughout the workflow.
Gotcha.
Very helpful.

I think going back to your question, how you would explain Fieldguide to somebody not in the industry, I think what we've consistently tried to do in our kind of brand...
Level of storytelling is we place our professional-grade agents for that agentic AI capability that Michelle talked about.
We place those sort of above the business units specifically.
So we have audit and advisory already in market, so like actual products for both of those segments, and we are launching tax quite soon.

So I think when we think about our agentic AI, we've really framed it around like professional-grade agents and kind of agentic AI that's built with domain expertise, kind of by practitioners for practitioners, and as such has quite a deep level of credibility that distinguishes itself from sort of cheap chatbots or copilots that are wrapped in really promising language, language but kind of don't do anything.
So I think that I that was probably the only thing that I would add.
Um...

Like we throw terms around internally that mean something when someone understands the context of them.

Gotcha.
Taking some notes here.
So I think we can.

I hinted on it before, maybe not so much of a subtle hint, but talking about these legacy solutions and point solutions that have kind of flooded the market or been there and trying to kind of piecemeal these together.
But I guess what was the kind of missing piece in the market before Fieldguide existed?
Is it really the kind of AI and agentic domain-based kind of embedded workflows?
Or can you just talk to me a little bit more kind of like origin story of why Fieldguide?
Yeah, I mean, I think I kind of voiced over that earlier.
I think the initial missing piece was an end-to-end platform.
So to kind of pick the picture before Fieldguide, firms will be using either like Thomson Reuters, Wolters Kluwer's caseware for their audit binder.
But they may be using

Using Shirlink as another example for their client portal where they're requesting evidence, requesting documents, exchanging files with their clients, which they then have to kind of bring back to their audit binder.
Or they could also be using like a data snipper for like the evidence documentation part, like reviewing evidence, reviewing documents, pulling data from documents.
so they're using like many different tools to supplement their audit binder because these legacy audit binders just didn't address some of these use cases.
And so I think the initial reason why Fieldguide existed is to kind of consolidate that end-to-end workflow into one platform.
But in addition to that, we didn't want to just build an end-to-end platform, but we wanted to change the way that these practitioners are working so that they spend less time.
On doing these very repetitive tasks, like reviewing a ton of documents, reviewing a bunch of evidence, and tying that back to the source of truth, and using AI to help with that.
Gotcha.
Yeah, and so I would say, Fieldguide is probably also the first to really offer AI for this industry, too.
So I heard a little bit of kind of simplifying workflows, saving time, and then, obviously, kind of AI, kind of keeping up with the movements in technology.
Kind of some core drivers, at least, that would resonate with some customers of kind of why would we look to switch from these monolithic or point solution systems to end-to-end platform?
Yeah, I think that that is...
For the practitioner, that's one element of it.
From a CISO or a CIO perspective, you're also, you're dealing with the risk that emerges when you have a really fragmented tech stack and the expenses associated with that.

When you have to maintain all of these different point solutions, it's expensive and it's also really risky because for every piece of fragmentation in a process, it introduces the chance for data to go missing, for mistakes to be introduced and kind of nobody knows where the mistakes kind of originated from.
So there's an element of risk reduction there.
And then just kind of back to how platforms, how end-to-end platforms sell themselves.
There's an element of how do we aggregate value across different teams and different functions within a firm's organization.

So when we unite audit, advisory, and tax, and we can get kind of that full firm adoption, what you start to get, again, under the premise of an aggregation of value, it is much more attractive for kind of mid-market.
For SMB and mid-market firms who want to simplify their tech stack and also just reduce the amount of vendors that they have to pay for and support.

When that vendor is one that has domain expert agentic capability that has proven metrics in different, that's built for an engagement workflow, but also has very tangible proven metrics across each stage of the audit advisory workflow in such that like 70% efficiency gains in this part of the process and, you know, 80 to 90% efficiency gains in another part of the process.
becomes like a very logical conclusion to draw.
Yeah, absolutely.

it kind of, I know we heard in the kickoff, like, partner is a very broad term because it can mean many different things.

So like kind of selling to more like CEO and like managing partner type personas.

To error, to reduce duplicative work, to reduce rework.

I think at the most basic level, Erik, the premise that we're operating on that we've built a lot of our strategic narratives around is that professional services generally is built on a foundation of trust.
Trust with the data, trust amongst teams that are extremely collaborative, and then ultimately everyone is accountable to the trust of the client.

So Fieldguide, I suppose, has built a platform around verifying and validating information to make you have trust in information, most importantly, such that let's say Michelle runs audit and I run tax.
Whatever I get from Michelle, I can trust that it is the most up-to-date.
It is not, you know, final version 1702, and I just missed the final version 1703, and somehow now all of my work is redundant.
There's trust in data that we work on, there's trust in the process, such that...
And because, again, our product was built by practitioners for practitioners.
It was built along the workflow of an auditor.

And I think that that in and of itself is a really attractive value proposition for buyers, both practitioner and kind of partner C-suite alike.
Yeah.

They want to grow the firm.

it.

all the above.
above.
When you think about the evolution in technology and how we are sort of every generation or so, you have a new professional baseline that's established.
know, that's how we kind of think of professional grade agents in the context of audit and advisory.
Like in the 90s, you had Excel and that became, it's a whole discipline where like you're not really serious, you're not really a serious business person, right?
If you don't know how to use Excel, if you don't know how to do VLOOKUPs in your sleep, or you're not like super into pivot tables, like you're not really seen as competent or serious.

And I guess the way we have likened professional grade agents in Fieldguide, the way we've sort of likened it to that is that when you have a discipline as a CPA and you're involved in audit or you're involved in, you know, advisory assurance work or tax work, there are tools that you begin to use that become a professional baseline and that's what we're, that's what we are sort of striving for when it comes to our product here at Fieldguide.
Okay.
Okay.
Thank
And in terms of what ultimately we're selling to our prospects, yeah, you nailed it.
It's increased capacity so your team can do more with the headcount that they have.
The quality is higher because they not only have the context of their own experience, but the corpus of work that Fieldguide is trained on, it's at this point, 27,500 audit engagements.
So you have best practice from across the best auditors in the world in how world-class audits and engagements are delivered and what, I suppose, what lessons learned there are.
So you have, in that context, I think, it is very much a case of time, capacity, unlocking additional capacity and generating more revenue because you don't have to increase headcount to...
Like commensurate with the growth goals you have.
So in that respect, I think it's keeping the story really simple, but really sharp.

Like for example, on a recent customer benchmark report that we did, we, through using Fieldguide, on two of their practice lines, high trust audits and SOC audits, from using Fieldguide, they would have been able to increase their incremental revenue that year.

Something like almost a million dollars across those two lines of business without adding any additional headcount, just because their people could be so much more productive using Fieldguide tools that were built for practitioners by practitioners.
Does that make sense?
So I think that that's really how we're trying to sell the platform.
Gotcha.
Wonderful.
Are there any trends that make Fieldguide kind of more relevant right now?
I think I've heard a little bit of kind of staffing constraints.
Yesterday, we heard a little bit of, you know, the CPA, people like going to take the test just weren't as, wasn't as popular as it has been in the past.
Some audit complexity, think, you know, different data coming in and out of different businesses.
But any specific trends that kind of just make you guys more relevant today?
Well, across the board, you have, you have more, you have more financial complexity, right?

Like, we live in a world where you have increased, you have a much more increased market for digital assets, for example, and reporting, like regulatory pressure for much more transparent reporting has also increased.
So in a world where you have, again, more pressure to report faster.
And the environment in which you operate has become more complex.
What got you here won't necessarily get you there.
So the overarching trend that we see across financial services, across professional services generally, is that technology needs to leapfrog in order to meet the market where it is.
So you have market pressures.
In terms of customer pressures, I used to have a product leader in PayPal that used to say this all the time, and it was just so powerful.
He used to say, as a PayPal, you weren't just competing with Visa or Stripe or Apple Wallet.
You were competing with Instagram, and you were competing with Walmart, and you were competing with Amazon.
Anywhere that people spend time outside their B2B lives changes the expectation they have on B2B products that they use in the context of their job.
What that means in the context of field guide is that we expect transparent processes.
We expect that if I am a client and my firm is doing it.
Or some sort of an engagement on my behalf.
I expect that that's not going to cost me, you know, 60 hours a week where I have to email them everything that I've ever done.
I would expect that engagements are done fast.
They're done transparently.
They're done in a way that doesn't disrupt my business, that they're done in a way that I can trust, that there is no scenario where it will be a burden to me.
So you have customer pressure.
From a competitive standpoint, then you have all of these firms who have very, very small margins between them.
You know, like the way that firms differentiate themselves in the context of professional services is really through longevity of relationship.
They can't be too, they can't really be super competitive on price because the price is the price and there can't be too much margin between them, you know, competitive A, B, and C.
And really the cost of goods sold, the cost of doing business, clients don't really care.
Clients don't really care what you use or what you don't use.
But like if one firm is coming to you and saying that an engagement is going to cost $100,000.
And another firm is saying that they'll do it for $400,000, like where is the delta there?
So there is an increasing pressure to be as competitive as possible, and technology is really the only way to fill that gap.

And again, back to the domain expertise of Fieldguide, it's like once you start to assess the environment that we're in, you start to realize that, you know, there is a lot of copycats where you have, you know, ChatGPT more or less wrapped in some flowery language, and that's not the same thing as an agentic tool that is expertly designed for auditors or for people in advisory or people in tax.
Does that make sense?
Yep, absolutely.
So I would say that that's it.

Michelle, would you have anything to add there aside from so you have like market pressures and that changing market dynamic, you have competitive pressures, and then you just have customer expectations and like very little margin to difference?
Yeah, and I would say the only thing I would add that comes to mind is on the audit and advisory side, there's just new types of work that they need to do.
So if you think of audits, traditionally, it's like auditing financial statements so that anyone who invests in a company can trust those financial numbers.
And then on the SOC, SOC, for instance, is kind of making sure that the technology that you're using has the right controls in place to make sure that your data is safe.
That your data is managed appropriately.
And then there's like other ones that are like HITRUST, that are specific for healthcare data, PCI, that are specific for like payments data.
But now, like again, like kind of what Dee mentioned with new technology, we have new assessments like, for instance, the newest one is like AI governance.
Like we, Fieldguide just got our own like ISO 42001.
So there's like new type of assessments that are coming up with new technology.
So that just means like new types of work that these practitioners need to learn how.
I do, but also, like, add to the services that they now provide.
So it's just, again, like, more work, more types of work.
And, like, it's really, and, yeah, I guess, like, the talent pipeline kind of plays into this that less people are entering the field.
So there's more work for them to do, newer types of work, but there's less people to do it.
And, like, also, I guess it's also transitioning to, like, less qualified people to do these types of work, too.
And so we're kind of, like, kind of, AI is kind of the solution of, like, meeting that gap.
Yep.
Gotcha.
All right.
If were to interview your three best customers, not your biggest, but best customers, what would they say Fieldguide is uniquely good at?
Outside of, we understand, like, the end-to-end platform and kind of agentic domain-based.
I just interviewed five customers, so let me know exactly.
And I could actually send you the video footage of them because it might actually give you a peek behind the curtain.
But I think the common threads across all of our customer interviews was that Fieldguide has built the tools that they wish they could build themselves.

And unlike generic tools, generic AI tools that, again, are not grounded in the domain specificity or like the nuances of audit, the nuances of advisory and tax, our customers would probably feel as though it's kind of the best of both worlds.
You have kind of expert built platform powered.
And the mix of those two things is a really, really strong value proposition.
So you just you have that domain expertise, I think, is first and foremost a huge differentiation.

Second is that for a lot of our customers, our best customers, not necessarily biggest, but best customers, we are super collaborative with our customers in the sense that, you know, having interviewed them, they're like, we pick up the phone and we feel like we have complete, we have not complete, but we have a huge influence on your roadmap.

We can, just by articulating our needs and what we need from our business perspective, that can translate into new features that you launch or new product lines that you support, and that in and of itself has helped us to grow.

And then I suppose, last but not least, and it's a point that Michelle has, Michelle brought up earlier on, it's that when we think about what differentiates or distinguishes our customers from one another, because it's an incredibly competitive market, every single tool that they use or
A or partner that they work with has to kind of really drive immense value for their end clients.
Because again, as we kind of already mentioned, margins are super tight.
So I think choosing tools or choosing partners like Fieldguide that enables their practitioners or their teammates to deliver higher quality work, higher quality work faster.
It really impacts the client relationship at the very end.
And that in and of itself has a direct line to revenue.
Michelle, would you add anything to that?
No, I think you definitely got the core things and trying to just think back to like any other customer conversations I've had and there's anything else to add, but nothing comes to mind right now.

Domain expert AI, for sure.
Across all of our products.
The ability for us to have professional grade agents that know what good looks like, right?
That's critical.
It knows what good looks like.
It can identify missing or mismatched data before it ever hits the team's desk and can flag to the client, hey, this is missing.
This needs to be rectified before we can do anything.

Again, having that, like, lingua franca or that, like, same language between two practice areas, let's say audit and tax, that need to work very close together but are using different tools, that's a huge operational inefficiency.
And again, a platform that's designed to bring these functions closer together is only ever going to be more beneficial for the end customer.
So I think that's probably another part of it.
Michelle, would you have anything to add?
Yeah, I just pulled up some notes from previous customer conversations.
Like one quote that stood out was one of our customers says, like, we chose Fieldguide because we need a partner as aggressive, flexible, and fast-paced as we are.
Anything that's a core differentiator specific, like, especially against, like, our legacy, like, sort of existing incumbent legacy tech partners that they have.

It's that we hear so often from our customers who come, or prospects that come to us, they've been in the industry for 20 years and they're like, the tech has not looked any different from 20 years ago.
And if these firms want to grow and survive in the next decade, they need someone who will be able to sort of match the speed at which they want to grow.

But also be flexible for their firm too because, you know, how these firms stand out from each other is the way that they approach their audits, the way that they provide sort of expertise to their clients.
So being able to also like be flexible to tailor to their specific methodologies and approaches is also very important to them.
And all the legacy techs are kind of known to be very like rigid, it's hard to customize, and it's just, they're just very slow to innovate.
So I would say that's another key differentiator.
Yeah, kind of like the legacy ones are, you have to adapt to how they work versus, you know, like were made specifically how you work today.

Yes, thank you for saying that because our customers have also said that is that like I don't have to fix my, I don't need to fit my processes into the tech, but like the tech is like the other way around, like the technology.
We can kind of mold into the way that we do our work.
Yeah.
We've got about seven minutes left, so I'm going to rapid fire some questions to get through some here.
But are there any features or workflows consistently that, like, wow customers during demos?
Agents, probably, right, Michelle?
Yeah, I would say the latest would be our agents, specifically our testing agent.
So these practitioners, they spend most of their time of their engagement doing, like, the testing part.
So kind of what you traditionally think, like, auditors would do, they would, like, look at the evidence, compare it, and see if there's any discrepancies, document all the results, test.
So that's, like, the testing part.
That could take, like, weeks, days, like, many, many hours if you're doing it all manually.
But now with our testing agent.

You could run all the tests at once in just a click of a button, and the agent would do all that sort of manual part of it, like comparing the data, documenting the results, and then as a result, the auditor is still doing their job, right?
They're still applying their expertise of reviewing, okay, here's the results of the test.
What does this mean?
Like, how does this affect what I do next in the audit?
So I would say, yeah, like that's, it would be the testing agent.
I think that's one of the more wowing features recently.
Yeah.
And I guess, is it, what about the agent?
Is it like reliability?
Is it speed?
Any kind of specific speed and accuracy?
Yeah.
Think of, think of it like extending, amplifying the impact of your best people, extending the judgment of your best auditors.
And again, the premise is, is that.
We're kind of like an Iron Man suit for auditors.
You don't want to necessarily replace human judgment.
That kind of human first story is really, really important to us in the dystopian sea of AI companies.
The whole premise is how can you build a tool that enables people to do what people do best and do what computers do best?
And as we've used that premise to say, okay, how can we do tools?
How can we build these tools that automate that which can be automated?
Like super necessary, really necessary, but very repetitive, mundane tasks across the engagement life cycle, like to Michelle's point, testing.
It is consistently the part of the engagement that takes the longest time by orders of magnitude.
How can we build something that can test something faster?
How can we build a tool that can spot?
How
Anomalies or errors quicker and get them rectified, such that you free up the humans in the loop to use their best judgment that they have deduced or they have gained over often decades in the field.
So it's kind of how we see it.
And it's a very human first story.
So I think that that is really important to us.
And then are there specific pain points or triggers that you guys hear outside of kind of tech stack complexity and not having access to domain-specific AI or agents?
Anything that really drives kind of, for us, like awareness stage content?
Yes.
So something that we invested in was called, and I'm sure you've heard it from other, from
Along your travels when you spoke to the Delanjen team is the premise of the AI maturity framework.
What we've consistently heard from customers is that, you know, because Fieldguide is more expensive than a lot of our competitors.
